subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.1.1 Demonstrate an understanding of quantities and place value for multi-digit whole numerals up to 100,000
indicator_description: Model number quantities, place value for multidigit- using graph sheets or multi-base materials up to 100,000
examplars:
1. Ask learners to model number quantities up to 100,000 using graph sheets or multi-base materials[cite: 1221].
2. Ask learners to model the number 12,500 shading graph sheet square where 2cm×2cm square represents 10 units[cite: 1221].
3. Give learners teacher-made token currency notes [¢1, ¢10 & ¢100 notes] and ask them to model or pick given amounts up to 10,000[cite: 1227].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.1.1 Demonstrate an understanding of quantities and place value for multi-digit whole numerals up to 100,000
indicator_description: Read and write numbers in figures and in words up 100,000
examplars:
1. Play the place value number wheel game to generate 6-digit numbers[cite: 1227].
2. Ask learners to write given numbers on the expanded form: 14,031 = 10,000 + 4,000 + 30 + 1[cite: 1230].
3. Display a number chart or number line with multiples of 500 to identify numbers in different positions around a given number[cite: 1230].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.1.1 Demonstrate an understanding of quantities and place value for multi-digit whole numerals up to 100,000
indicator_description: Identify numbers in different positions around a given number in a number chart
examplars: 1. Display a number chart in multiples of 500 between 10,000 and 100,000 and have learners identify numbers in different positions around a chosen number[cite: 1233].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.1.1 Demonstrate an understanding of quantities and place value for multi-digit whole numerals up to 100,000
indicator_description: Compare and order whole numbers up to 10,000 and represent comparisons using the symbols "<", "=", ">"
examplars:
1. Identify numbers which are 1,000 and 10,000 more or less than a given number[cite: 1233].
2. Work together in groups to order a given set of numbers in ascending or descending order[cite: 1233].
3. Give two numbers between 10,000 and 100,000 and encourage learners to say as many things as possible about the two numbers[cite: 1233].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.1.1 Demonstrate an understanding of quantities and place value for multi-digit whole numerals up to 100,000
indicator_description: Round (off, up, down) whole numbers up to 10,000 to the nearest, thousands, hundreds and tens
examplars:
1. Round off numbers to the nearest 1000, 100 and 10[cite: 1236].
2. Round up and round down to estimate sums and differences[cite: 1236].
3. Work together to round up and round down to estimate sums (e.g. 230+160 is approx 400)[cite: 1236].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.1.1 Demonstrate an understanding of quantities and place value for multi-digit whole numerals up to 100,000
indicator_description: Skip count forwards and backwards in 50s and 100s up to and from 10,000
examplars:
1. Put learners into groups where one mentions a number and another makes a skip count in 50s or 100s[cite: 1239].
2. Skip count forwards and backwards and challenge learners to identify or correct errors or omissions[cite: 1239].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.1.2 Demonstrate understanding of Roman numerals up XXX (30)
indicator_description: Develop an understanding of Roman Numeral system up XXX (i.e. 30)
examplars:
1. Display Roman numeral charts (1-30) and lead learners to identify characters I, II, III, IV, V, X[cite: 1239].
2. Match Roman numerals to Hindu-Arabic numerals (e.g. I=1, V=5, X=10)[cite: 1239].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.1.2 Demonstrate understanding of Roman numerals up XXX (30)
indicator_description: Count and convert Hindu Arabic numerals to Roman numerals up to 30 and vice versa
examplars:
1. Read the chart sequentially forwards, backwards, vertically, and diagonally[cite: 1242].
2. Convert Hindu Arabic system to Roman numeration (e.g. 24 = XXIV) and vice versa[cite: 1242].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.1.3 Demonstrate an understanding of factors, multiples and squared numbers
indicator_description: Determine set of factors of a given numbers up to 50
examplars:
1. Make formation of sets of equal objects from a given quantity (e.g. with 12 straws)[cite: 1245].
2. Pair grouping and the number of equal objects for each formation as factors[cite: 1245].
3. Collect from pairs and form a set as the factors (e.g. factors of 12 = {1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12})[cite: 1245].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.1.3 Demonstrate an understanding of factors, multiples and squared numbers
indicator_description: Determine the highest common factor (HCF) of any two whole numbers between 1 and 50
examplars:
1. List factors of two given whole numbers and determine the set of common factors and the HCF[cite: 1248].
2. Use Venn diagrams to find the common factors and the HCF by placing factors in regions[cite: 1248].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.1.3 Demonstrate an understanding of factors, multiples and squared numbers
indicator_description: Determine the lowest common multiple (LCM) of at least any two given numbers up to 100
examplars:
1. Identify and list the set of multiples using skip counting[cite: 1248].
2. List the set of common multiples of two given numbers and identify the LCM[cite: 1248].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.1.3 Demonstrate an understanding of factors, multiples and squared numbers
indicator_description: Recognise the relationship between factors and multiples
examplars:
1. Break given multiples into factors and pairs of factors[cite: 1251].
2. Investigate even and odd numbers[cite: 1251].
3. Investigate numbers that are multiples of 2, 3, 4 and 5 using divisibility tests[cite: 1251].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.1.3 Demonstrate an understanding of factors, multiples and squared numbers
indicator_description: Generate and analyse patterns in square numbers
examplars:
1. Use bottle tops to generate square numbers (1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36...)[cite: 1251].
2. Investigate the relationship between square numbers and the sum of consecutive odd numbers[cite: 1254].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.1.3 Demonstrate an understanding of factors, multiples and squared numbers
indicator_description: Represent square numbers using factors
examplars:
1. Draw a square whose side is 1x1, 2x2, 3x3 to determine area[cite: 1254].
2. Work in groups to continue the pattern to include factors of 144[cite: 1254].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.1.4 Interpret negative and positive numbers in context
indicator_description: Describe real life situations using positive and negative values
examplars: 1. Brainstorm happenings represented with positive and negative numbers (e.g. savings and debt, profit and loss)[cite: 1257].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.1.4 Interpret negative and positive numbers in context
indicator_description: Count forwards and backwards with positive and negative whole numbers through zero
examplars: 1. Count from a given interval on the number line (e.g. from 10 backward through zero to -8)[cite: 1257].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.2.1 Recall basic multiplication fact up to 12x12
indicator_description: Determine basic multiplication facts up to 12x12
examplars:
1. Arrange straws vertically (legs) and horizontally (arms) and count intersections[cite: 1260].
2. Model multiplication sentences using dots[cite: 1260].
3. Play the 3-in-a-line game using a 6x6 multiplication chart[cite: 1260].
4. Find doubles and squares of given numbers and skip count[cite: 1260].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.2.2 Describe and apply mental mathematics strategies and number properties to determine answers for basic multiplication facts to 81 and related division facts
indicator_description: Apply mental mathematics strategies and number properties to determine answers for basic multiplication facts to 81 and related division facts
examplars:
1. Describe strategies such as skip counting up/down from known facts, doubling, patterns for 9s, repeated doubling/halving, and relating division to multiplication[cite: 1264].
2. Recall multiplication facts to 81 and related division facts[cite: 1264].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.2.2 Describe and apply mental mathematics strategies and number properties to determine answers for basic multiplication facts to 81 and related division facts
indicator_description: Apply mental mathematics strategies for multiplication, such as annexing then adding zero halving and doubling using the distributive property
examplars:
1. Determine products by annexing or adding zeros for multiples of 10, 100, or 1000[cite: 1267].
2. Apply halving and doubling when determining a product (e.g. 32x5 is 16x10)[cite: 1267].
3. Apply distributive property for products close to multiples of 10[cite: 1267].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.2.3 Demonstrate an understanding of multiplication (2 or 3-digit by 1digit-)
indicator_description: Multiply multidigit -numbers efficiently
examplars:
1. Use the "expand and box" method (partial decomposition) for multi-digit multiplication[cite: 1267].
2. Use the lattice method for solving products like 54x3[cite: 1267].
3. Use 2x2 rectangular boxes for solving products like 34x17[cite: 1267].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.2.4 Recall basic division fact up to 100
indicator_description: Determine basic division fact up to 81
examplars:
1. Investigate divisibility tests for 2, 3, 4 and 5[cite: 1270].
2. Play the 3-in-a-line game using a 6x6 multiplication chart to identify quotients[cite: 1270].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.2.5 Demonstrate an understanding of division (2- or 3-digit by one digit number)
indicator_description: Divide 2-digit numbers by I-digit number efficiently
examplars:
1. Division as repeated subtraction using the long division method[cite: 1270].
2. Division using the estimation of multiples of 10, 100 (nicknamed the "Big 7")[cite: 1273].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.2.6 Translate and solve word problems involving the four basic operations on whole numbers
indicator_description: Solve multi-step word problems involving the four basic operations
examplars:
1. Model mathematical statements from word problems involving addition and multiplication[cite: 1273].
2. Model statements involving division and subtraction[cite: 1273].
3. Role play word problems involving multiple operations[cite: 1273].
4. Use calculators and spreadsheets to assess the reasonableness of answers[cite: 1273].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.3.1 Develop an understanding of equivalent and improper fractions
indicator_description: Generate unit fractions and locate a unit fraction, e.g. one-eighth, on a number line
examplars:
1. Use number line to locate one eighth by partitioning the interval from 0 to 1 into 8 equal parts[cite: 1276].
2. Partition lines into unit fractions 1/2, 1/3, 1/5, 1/6, 1/10[cite: 1276].
3. Use drawn number charts to read equivalent fractions[cite: 1276].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.3.1 Demonstrate an understanding of strategies for comparing, adding and subtracting fractions
indicator_description: Recognise and name equivalent fractions using pictorial representations and number line to determine the Lowest Common Denominator (LCD)
examplars:
1. Use fraction chart to illustrate one-half is 2-fourths, 3-sixths, or 4-eighths[cite: 1280].
2. Illustrate relationships by observing how numerators and denominators are multiplied by common units[cite: 1280].
3. Colour equivalent fractions or write equivalents for given fractions[cite: 1280].
4. Use LCD to express equivalent fractions that are comparable[cite: 1280].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.3.1 Demonstrate an understanding of strategies for comparing, adding and subtracting fractions
indicator_description: Find the simplest form of given fractions by dividing through by the highest common factor
examplars:
1. Use pictorial representations to illustrate simplest form (e.g. 6/12 = 1/2)[cite: 1284].
2. Divide through by the highest common factor[cite: 1284].
3. Express numerator and denominators as factors and cancel common factors[cite: 1284].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.3.1 Demonstrate an understanding of strategies for comparing, adding and subtracting fractions
indicator_description: Recognise fractions that are greater than one (i.e. improper fractions), draw and label such fractions with their symbols
examplars:
1. Use pictorial representations to introduce improper fractions where the numerator is larger than the denominator[cite: 1287].
2. Express mixed fractions as improper fractions and draw/colour them[cite: 1287].
3. Use number lines to identify mixed and improper fractions[cite: 1287].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.3.2 Demonstrate an understanding of strategies for comparing, adding and subtracting fractions
indicator_description: Compare and order fractions with like denominators by using pictorial representations and finding equivalent fractions using the Lowest Common Denominator (LCD)
examplars:
1. Use number line or fraction chart to illustrate comparable fractional units[cite: 1290].
2. Use number line or charts to compare and order unit fractions[cite: 1290].
3. Find LCD to arrange fractions from largest to smallest[cite: 1290].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.3.2 Demonstrate an understanding of strategies for comparing, adding and subtracting fractions
indicator_description: Provide examples of where fractions are used
examplars:
1. Discuss real life contexts: selling liquids, sharing pizza/cake, buying objects in packets (half crates), and sewing[cite: 1293].
2. Tell time by half/quarter past and to[cite: 1293].
3. Draw circle graphs representing halves, fourths and eighths[cite: 1293].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.4.1 Develop an understanding of decimals (tenths and hundredths) using concrete and pictorial representations and write decimal fractions as base ten numbers with place value.
indicator_description: Describe and represent decimals (tenths and hundredths) concretely, pictorially, and symbolically
examplars:
1. Use pictorial representations or number line to identify decimal fractions[cite: 1296].
2. Use place value charts to write fractions as base ten numbers with a decimal point[cite: 1296].
3. Convert common fractions into tenths and hundredths and write decimal names[cite: 1296].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.4.1 Develop an understanding of decimals (tenths and hundredths) using concrete and pictorial representations and write decimal fractions as base ten numbers with place value.
indicator_description: Round decimals to the nearest tenth
examplars:
1. Explain rules of rounding decimals similar to whole numbers[cite: 1299].
2. Use tables to practice rounding decimal fractions to nearest tenths or hundredths[cite: 1299].
3. Change fractions to decimals and write results to the nearest tenths or hundredths[cite: 1299].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.4.1 Develop an understanding of decimals (tenths and hundredths) using concrete and pictorial representations and write decimal fractions as base ten numbers with place value.
indicator_description: Use models to explain the result of addition and subtraction of decimals (up to hundredths)
examplars:
1. Add decimals by aligning place values (e.g. 0.64 + 0.39)[cite: 1302].
2. Add tenths and hundredths by lining up decimal points (e.g. 0.6 + 0.39)[cite: 1302].
3. Subtract decimals by lining up decimal points to align place values[cite: 1302].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.5.1 Demonstrate an understanding of percent (limited to whole numbers) concretely, pictorially, and symbolically)
indicator_description: Model or recognise percent (as a fraction related to hundredths) using concrete models, pictorial representations and number line.
examplars:
1. Use pictorial representations or number line to introduce hundredths and identify fractions[cite: 1305].
2. Use long division to convert and write fractions as decimals[cite: 1305].
3. Convert common fractions into hundredths and write decimal names or percentages (e.g. 3/4 = 75%)[cite: 1305].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.1.5.1 Demonstrate an understanding of percent (limited to whole numbers) concretely, pictorially, and symbolically)
indicator_description: Compare and order a mixture of common, decimal and percent fractions (up to hundredths)
examplars:
1. Express mixture in one form (common, decimal, or percent) to compare and order[cite: 1308].
2. Locate fractions on the number line to order them[cite: 1308].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.2.1.1 Demonstrate an understanding of how to identify and describe patterns found in tables and charts, including a multiplication chart
indicator_description: Describe the pattern found in a given table or chart
examplars: 1. Use hundred charts to colour numbers during skip counting and describe the patterns created[cite: 1313].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.2.1.1 Demonstrate an understanding of how to identify and describe patterns found in tables and charts, including a multiplication chart
indicator_description: Determine the missing element(s) in a given table or chart
examplars: 1. Identify missing numbers in charts and explain reasoning[cite: 1313].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.2.1.1 Demonstrate an understanding of how to identify and describe patterns found in tables and charts, including a multiplication chart
indicator_description: Identify the error(s) in a given table or chart
examplars: 1. Provide mapping tables containing errors and ask students to identify and explain them[cite: 1313].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.2.1.2 Translate among different representations of a pattern, such as a table, a chart or concrete material
indicator_description: Create a concrete representation of a given pattern displayed in a table or chart
examplars: 1. Complete tables and create concrete representations using linking cubes or other manipulatives[cite: 1316].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.2.1.2 Translate among different representations of a pattern, such as a table, a chart or concrete material
indicator_description: Create a table or chart from a given concrete representation of a pattern.
examplars: 1. Present geometric design series and record them in T-charts, predicting the 10th, 12th, or 20th steps[cite: 1316].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.2.1.3 Represent, describe and extend patterns and relationships, using charts and tables, to solve problems
indicator_description: Translate the information in a given problem into a table or chart
examplars:
1. Extend numerical patterns and explain the determined pattern and missing elements[cite: 1319].
2. Use manipulatives to represent patterns and describe chart-to-concrete relationships[cite: 1319].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.2.1.3 Represent, describe and extend patterns and relationships, using charts and tables, to solve problems
indicator_description: Identify and extend the patterns in a table or chart to solve a given problem.
examplars:
1. Compare two related tables, extending them and explaining similarities and differences[cite: 1319].
2. Challenge other students to discover pattern relationships in student-made pairs[cite: 1319].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.2.2.1 Express a given problem as an equation in which a symbol is used to represent an unknown number
indicator_description: Write a given problem as an equation in which a symbol is used to represent an unknown number
examplars: 1. Explain the purpose of box symbols in equations (e.g. [] + 5 = 8)[cite: 1322].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.2.2.1 Express a given problem as an equation in which a symbol is used to represent an unknown number
indicator_description: Express a given pictorial or concrete representation of an equation in symbolic form
examplars: 1. Write equations with unknowns for situations involving perimeter or collections of objects[cite: 1322].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.2.2.2 Solve one-step equations involving a symbol to represent an unknown number
indicator_description: Solve a given one-step equation using manipulatives
examplars: 1. Use pan balances to solve simple equations like 7 + [] = 12[cite: 1326].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.2.2.2 Solve one-step equations involving a symbol to represent an unknown number
indicator_description: Describe orally, the meaning of a given one-step equation with one unknown.
examplars: 1. Solve equations like triangle - 13 = 20 and explain the thinking[cite: 1326].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.2.2.2 Solve one-step equations involving a symbol to represent an unknown number
indicator_description: Solve a given equation when the unknown is on the left or right side of the equation.
examplars: 1. Use base ten materials to solve equations like 109 + [] = 164[cite: 1326].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.2.2.2 Solve one-step equations involving a symbol to represent an unknown number
indicator_description: Solve a given one-step equation using "guess and check"
examplars: 1. Place numbers in symbols to make number sentences true (e.g. triangle - 7 = 6)[cite: 1326].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.2.2.2 Solve one-step equations involving a symbol to represent an unknown number
indicator_description: Identify the unknown in a problem, represent the problem with an equation, and solve the problem concretely, pictorially or symbolically
examplars: 1. Use linking cubes to model and solve comparison problems (e.g. comparing red and blue marble counts)[cite: 1326].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.2.2.2 Solve one-step equations involving a symbol to represent an unknown number
indicator_description: Represent and solve a given addition or subtraction problem involving a "part-part-whole" or comparison context, using a symbol to represent the unknown
examplars: 1. Solve age difference problems and class attendance problems[cite: 1329].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.2.2.2 Solve one-step equations involving a symbol to represent an unknown number
indicator_description: Create a problem for a given equation with one unknown.
examplars: 1. Create matching story problems for equations like 14 + [] = 21 and solve them[cite: 1329].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.3.1.1 Identify the lines of symmetry of regular and irregular 2D shapes
indicator_description: Complete drawings of shapes to make them symmetrical
examplars:
1. Use graph sheets to complete incomplete 2D shapes to make symmetrical pictures[cite: 1333].
2. Use worksheets with incomplete 2D shapes and marked lines of symmetry[cite: 1333].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.3.1.1 Identify the lines of symmetry of regular and irregular 2D shapes
indicator_description: Identify the lines of symmetry of regular and irregular 2D shapes (triangles and quadrilateral)
examplars:
1. Sort symmetrical and non-symmetrical objects[cite: 1333].
2. Fold 2D shapes to investigate the number of lines of symmetry[cite: 1336].
3. Copy 2D shapes and draw their lines of symmetry[cite: 1336].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.3.2.1 Describe the position of objects in space using the cardinal points
indicator_description: Tell the position and motion of objects in space using the cardinal points north, south, east and west
examplars:
1. Discuss worksheets on graphs with labeled and unlabeled axes[cite: 1339].
2. Describe position and motion (e.g. Point P is west of point T but north of A)[cite: 1339].
3. Locate coordinates (ordered pairs) of points on a grid[cite: 1339].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.3.3.1 Demonstrate understanding of perimeter of regular and irregular shapes
indicator_description: Estimate perimeter using referents for centimetre or metre
examplars: 1. Estimate and calculate actual perimeter of objects using referents, then measure with tape/ruler to compare[cite: 1344].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.3.3.1 Demonstrate understanding of perimeter of regular and irregular shapes
indicator_description: Measure and record perimeter for regular and irregular shapes in cm and m.
examplars:
1. Use a ruler to measure all sides of a regular shape and sum results[cite: 1344].
2. Explore area of irregular cut-out shapes by tracing on graph sheets and counting unit squares[cite: 1344].
3. Sum the given sizes of an irregular shape to obtain perimeter[cite: 1347].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.3.3.1 Demonstrate understanding of perimeter of regular and irregular shapes
indicator_description: Develop and apply a formula for determining perimeter of square and rectangle
examplars:
1. Demonstrate perimeter using cut-out shapes[cite: 1347].
2. Measure sides of a rectangular cut-out to determine perimeter using AB + AD + BC + CD[cite: 1347].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.3.3.1 Demonstrate understanding of perimeter of regular and irregular shapes
indicator_description: Construct different rectangles for a given perimeter (cm, m) to demonstrate that many shapes are possible for a perimeter.
examplars: 1. Draw three different rectangular shapes for the same perimeter (e.g. 36cm)[cite: 1350].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.3.3.2 Demonstrate an understanding of area of regular and irregular 2-D shapes
indicator_description: Recognise that area is measured in square units.
examplars:
1. Explore area of exercise books using 1cm by 1cm square sheets[cite: 1350].
2. Calculate area of squares on a graph sheet and count total squares[cite: 1350].
3. Measure area of a floor tile and multiply by the number of tiles[cite: 1350].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.3.3.2 Demonstrate an understanding of area of regular and irregular 2-D shapes
indicator_description: Select and justify referents for the units cm² or m²
examplars: 1. Compare area explorations of smaller objects (books) vs larger objects (classrooms) and justify unit choice[cite: 1350].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.3.3.2 Demonstrate an understanding of area of regular and irregular 2-D shapes
indicator_description: Estimate area by using referents for cm² or m²
examplars:
1. Estimate item areas in the classroom and compare with actual measurements[cite: 1353].
2. Use an exercise book area as a reference to estimate teacher table area[cite: 1353].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.3.3.2 Demonstrate an understanding of area of regular and irregular 2-D shapes
indicator_description: Develop and apply a formula for determining area of a rectangle and square
examplars:
1. Draw squares on graph sheets and find area by counting units[cite: 1353].
2. Count cubic squares on vertical sections and multiply by horizontal sections[cite: 1353].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.3.3.2 Demonstrate an understanding of area of regular and irregular 2-D shapes
indicator_description: Construct different rectangles for a given area (cm² or m²) in order to demonstrate that many different rectangles may have the same area.
examplars: 1. Draw three different rectangles for the same area (e.g. 24cm²)[cite: 1353].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.3.3.3 Demonstrate understanding of time taken by events in minutes and hours
indicator_description: Tell the time in hours and minutes in analogue and digital watches including 24-hour clocks
examplars:
1. Use analogue clocks to tell time using quarter past, half past, quarter to, and 12 o'clock marks[cite: 1356].
2. Use various hour hand positions to tell different times[cite: 1356].
3. Sketch clock faces to tell given times[cite: 1356].
4. Explore relationships in units (60s = 1min, 60min = 1hr)[cite: 1360].
5. Observe digital watch faces to tell time[cite: 1360].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.3.3.3 Demonstrate understanding of time taken by events in minutes and hours
indicator_description: Use clock to measure time to complete simple events in minutes and seconds
examplars:
1. Use clocks to record time taken to walk to the canteen/office[cite: 1360].
2. Use digital watches to record time for short actions like walking to a cupboard[cite: 1360].
3. Tell time taken for daily activities like brushing teeth or cooking[cite: 1360].

subject: Mathematics
content_standard: B4.3.3.3 Demonstrate understanding of time taken by events in minutes and hours
indicator_description: State dates of events and record calendar dates in a variety of formats
examplars:
1. Record dates in multiple formats (e.g. 28-Aug-18, 28/08/2018)[cite: 1360].
2. Write dates for national holidays (Independence Day, Founders' Day)[cite: 1360].
3. State birth dates and put them in a table to draw a time line-graph[cite: 1363].

subject: Mathematics
sub_strand: Data Collection, Organization, Presentation, Interpretation and Analysis
content_standard: B4.4.1.1 Demonstrate an understanding of many-to-one correspondence in displaying, and reading or interpreting, graphs
indicator_description: Use an understanding of one-to-one correspondence to read and interpret graphs
examplars: 1. Use graphs displaying favourite fruits where each box represents one pupil[cite: 1372].

subject: Mathematics
sub_strand: Data Collection, Organization, Presentation, Interpretation and Analysis
content_standard: B4.4.1.1 Demonstrate an understanding of many-to-one correspondence in displaying, and reading or interpreting, graphs
indicator_description: Use an understanding of many-to-one correspondence to display or construct graphs
examplars:
1. Discuss required objects/cut-outs if one symbol represents 2 pupils for an illness graph[cite: 1375].
2. Construct illness graphs by pasting correct cut-outs in columns[cite: 1375].
3. Read and interpret graphs with keys[cite: 1379].
4. Use tables to construct graphs requiring many-to-one correspondence[cite: 1379].

subject: Mathematics
sub_strand: Data Collection, Organization, Presentation, Interpretation and Analysis
content_standard: B4.4.1.1 Demonstrate an understanding of many-to-one correspondence in displaying, and reading or interpreting, graphs
indicator_description: Compare graphs in which the same data has been displayed and explain how they are the same and different
examplars: 1. Explain similarities and differences in graphs displaying the same data set (e.g. animal farm counts)[cite: 1381].

subject: Mathematics
sub_strand: Data Collection, Organization, Presentation, Interpretation and Analysis
content_standard: B4.4.1.1 Demonstrate an understanding of many-to-one correspondence in displaying, and reading or interpreting, graphs
indicator_description: Find examples of graphs in which many-to-one correspondence is used in print and electronic media, such as newspapers, magazines and the Internet, and describe the correspondence used
examplars: 1. Use many-to-one correspondence graphs from media (e.g. school population or breath-holding times) to read and interpret data[cite: 1384].

subject: Mathematics
sub_strand: Data Collection, Organization, Presentation, Interpretation and Analysis
content_standard: B4.4.1.2: Construct and interpret pictographs and bar graphs involving many-to-one correspondence to draw conclusions.
indicator_description: Identify common features of graphs that use many-to-one correspondence and use that understanding to draw bar graphs or pictographs, complete with title, labelled axes, key or legend, to represent data collected (up to 4 categories of data).
examplars:
1. Examine features: title, axes labels, key or legend[cite: 1387].
2. Draw bar graphs from tables (e.g. rainfall records) complete with labels and keys[cite: 1387].
3. Draw graphs for household energy usage and interpret results[cite: 1387].

subject: Mathematics
sub_strand: Data Collection, Organization, Presentation, Interpretation and Analysis
content_standard: B4.4.1.2: Construct and interpret pictographs and bar graphs involving many-to-one correspondence to draw conclusions
indicator_description: Use understanding of many-to-one correspondence to solve simple problems (how many altogether, comparing, or take apart problems) requiring interpretation of many-to-one bar graphs (up to 4 categories of data).
examplars: 1. Read and interpret graphs regarding medical treatments and school attendance[cite: 1390].
